title: Mastering Data Structures & Algorithms
tagline: Go from zero to confidently solving any interview-grade DSA problem.
description: A complete, problem-first path through Data Structures and Algorithms for programmers preparing for technical interviews, competitive programming, or just deeper engineering skill. Every stage pairs a concept with an interactive VisuAlgo animation, written references (cp-algorithms, GeeksforGeeks, NeetCode), and a hand-picked set of LeetCode problems so you learn the idea and immediately drill it. Work top to bottom — each stage assumes the previous ones.
outcome: You can recognize the right data structure or algorithm for an unfamiliar problem, implement it correctly from scratch, reason about its time and space complexity, and solve medium-to-hard LeetCode problems under time pressure.

stages.title: Foundations: Complexity & Problem-Solving
stages.summary: Before touching any structure, you learn to measure how good a solution is. This stage builds the Big-O mindset and the habit of analyzing time and space — the lens you'll use for everything that follows.
objectives: Express the time and space complexity of any loop, recursion, or built-in operation in Big-O notation.
Compare two solutions and explain which scales better and why.
Set up a clean coding workflow in one language (Python, Java, C++, or JavaScript).
mistakes: Memorizing Big-O of common operations without understanding where the cost comes from.
Optimizing constants instead of the asymptotic order of growth.
Skipping complexity analysis and jumping straight to code.

tasks.title: Learn asymptotic analysis and Big-O notation
tasks.summary: Understand order of growth, Big-O / Theta / Omega, and how to count operations.
tasks.whatYouGain: A precise vocabulary for 'how fast' and 'how much memory' that you'll apply to every problem for the rest of the roadmap.
details: Read the analysis section end to end and re-derive each example yourself.
Memorize the cost of common operations: array index, hash lookup, sort, append.
Practice writing the Big-O of nested loops and simple recursions by hand.

tasks.title: Set up your language and a daily practice loop
tasks.summary: Pick one language, set up a LeetCode account, and establish a repeatable solve-review rhythm.
tasks.whatYouGain: A frictionless environment so the only thing you spend energy on is thinking, not tooling.
details: Choose ONE language and stick with it for the whole roadmap.
Create a LeetCode account and bookmark the LeetCode 75 study plan as your warm-up track.
Write a tiny template (read input, call function, print output) you can reuse.

tasks.title: Warm-up problem set
tasks.summary: Solve five gentle problems to lock in the workflow and start the analysis habit.
tasks.whatYouGain: Confidence that your setup works and a first taste of stating complexity before and after coding.
details: For each problem, write the brute-force Big-O first, then your final Big-O.
Re-read the editorial even when you pass — note one idea you didn't think of.

resources.label: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock

stages.title: Arrays, Strings, Two Pointers & Sliding Window
stages.summary: Arrays are the substrate of almost every problem. Here you master the two highest-leverage array techniques — two pointers and sliding window — plus prefix sums, which turn many O(n^2) ideas into O(n).
objectives: Solve subarray and substring problems with sliding windows in linear time.
Use two pointers to scan sorted data and partition arrays in place.
Apply prefix sums to answer range-sum queries instantly.
caseStudies: Sliding window converts 'longest substring without repeats' from a nested-loop scan into a single pass — the canonical efficiency win.
mistakes: Forgetting to shrink the window when a constraint breaks.
Off-by-one errors at window or pointer boundaries.
Reaching for extra arrays when two indices would do.

tasks.title: Array fundamentals and visualization
tasks.summary: Review how arrays store data contiguously and watch operations animate.
tasks.whatYouGain: An intuition for why indexing is O(1) and insertion in the middle is O(n).
details: Play with the VisuAlgo Array module to see access, insert, and remove costs.
Read the GeeksforGeeks array guide for language-specific gotchas.

tasks.title: Learn two pointers, sliding window, and prefix sums
tasks.summary: The three patterns that solve most array and string problems efficiently.
tasks.whatYouGain: A reusable mental checklist: when you see 'subarray', 'substring', or 'sorted', you'll know which pattern to try first.

tasks.title: Two-pointer and sliding-window problem set
tasks.summary: Drill the patterns until the window-management code becomes automatic.
tasks.whatYouGain: Fluency in linear-time scanning — the technique you'll reuse constantly.
details: Name the pattern out loud before coding each one.
Track your window's invariant in a comment as you write it.

stages.title: Hashing & Hash Tables
stages.summary: Hash maps and sets trade memory for near-constant lookups, collapsing many quadratic solutions to linear. You learn how hashing works under the hood and when O(1) average is and isn't true.
objectives: Use hash maps and sets to deduplicate, count, and index data in O(1) average time.
Explain collision handling (chaining vs open addressing) at a high level.
Recognize the 'hash it to look it up later' pattern across problem types.
mistakes: Assuming O(1) worst case — adversarial keys can degrade hashing.
Using a hash map when a frequency array or two pointers is simpler.
Mutating keys after insertion.

tasks.title: How hash tables work
tasks.summary: See hashing, load factor, and collision resolution animated, then read the theory.
tasks.whatYouGain: A clear model of why hashing is fast on average and how it can break down.
details: Use the VisuAlgo Hash Table module to watch linear/quadratic probing resolve collisions.
Read the GeeksforGeeks hashing section for the chaining-vs-probing trade-offs.

tasks.title: Hash map / hash set problem set
tasks.summary: Solve five problems where a map or set is the unlock.
tasks.whatYouGain: The reflex to ask 'what if I'd already seen this value?' — the heart of hashing problems.

resources.label: Two Sum II - Input Array Is Sorted

stages.title: Linked Lists
stages.summary: Pointers made visible. Linked lists teach you to manipulate references carefully — the fast/slow pointer trick, in-place reversal, and cycle detection are interview staples built here.
objectives: Reverse, merge, and reorder linked lists in place.
Detect and locate cycles with Floyd's fast/slow pointers.
Use a dummy head node to simplify edge cases.
mistakes: Losing the rest of the list by reassigning a next pointer too early.
Not handling empty lists or single-node lists.
Forgetting to advance pointers, causing infinite loops.

tasks.title: Linked list mechanics, visualized
tasks.summary: Watch singly, doubly, and circular lists update as nodes are inserted and removed.
tasks.whatYouGain: A concrete picture of nodes and pointers so reference juggling stops feeling abstract.
details: Step through insert/delete on the VisuAlgo Linked List module.
Note how a dummy/sentinel node removes special cases.

tasks.title: Linked list problem set
tasks.summary: Five problems covering reversal, merging, cycles, and reordering.
tasks.whatYouGain: Steady hands with pointers and mastery of the fast/slow technique.
details: Draw the list on paper before coding each pointer move.
Test on empty, one-node, and two-node inputs.

resources.label: Remove Nth Node From End of List

stages.title: Stacks & Queues
stages.summary: Two simple structures with outsized power. Stacks unlock monotonic-stack and parsing problems; queues (and deques) power BFS and sliding-window maxima later on.
objectives: Use a stack for matching, parsing, and 'next greater element' problems.
Apply the monotonic stack pattern.
Understand queue/deque behavior for BFS and windowed extremes.
mistakes: Popping from an empty stack without a guard.
Confusing when to push the index versus the value.
Using a list as a queue with O(n) pops from the front.

tasks.title: Stack and queue behavior
tasks.summary: Review LIFO/FIFO semantics and the deque, using the linked-list module's stack/queue views.
tasks.whatYouGain: Clarity on which end you push and pop, and why deques matter for windows.

tasks.title: Learn the monotonic stack pattern
tasks.summary: A stack that stays sorted as you push — the key to 'next greater/smaller' problems.
tasks.whatYouGain: A pattern that turns several O(n^2) scan problems into a single O(n) pass.

tasks.title: Stack & queue problem set
tasks.summary: Five problems from parentheses matching up to histogram areas.
tasks.whatYouGain: Comfort with stacks for parsing and the monotonic-stack trick.

stages.title: Recursion & Backtracking
stages.summary: Recursion is the gateway to trees, graphs, and dynamic programming. Backtracking is recursion that explores choices and undoes them — the engine for subsets, permutations, and constraint puzzles.
objectives: Write recursive solutions with clear base and recursive cases.
Generate subsets, permutations, and combinations via backtracking.
Prune search trees to avoid exploring dead ends.
mistakes: Missing or wrong base case causing infinite recursion.
Forgetting to undo a choice (the 'backtrack' step).
Mutating shared state across recursive branches.

tasks.title: See recursion as a tree
tasks.summary: Visualize the call tree / DAG of a recursion and connect it to the call stack.
tasks.whatYouGain: The ability to picture recursive calls branching and returning — essential for trees and DP.
details: Use the VisuAlgo Recursion module to watch a recursion tree expand and collapse.
Trace a small factorial and Fibonacci by hand alongside it.

tasks.title: Backtracking problem set
tasks.summary: Five problems that build the choose / explore / un-choose muscle.
tasks.whatYouGain: A reusable backtracking template you can adapt to almost any 'generate all valid X' problem.
details: Write the template once: choose, recurse, un-choose.
Add pruning only after a correct brute-force version works.

stages.title: Searching & Sorting
stages.summary: Binary search is the most underused interview tool — it shows up far beyond sorted arrays. You also learn the major sorts and, crucially, 'binary search on the answer'.
objectives: Implement binary search bug-free, including on rotated and 2D arrays.
Apply binary search on a value range, not just an index.
Explain merge sort and quicksort and their complexities.
caseStudies: Koko Eating Bananas reframes a speed question as binary search over possible speeds — the 'search the answer' pattern.
mistakes: Infinite loops from wrong mid/low/high updates.
Integer overflow in mid computation in some languages.
Not confirming the search space is monotonic before binary searching it.

tasks.title: Sorting algorithms, animated
tasks.summary: Watch bubble, insertion, merge, quick, counting, and radix sort run side by side.
tasks.whatYouGain: Intuition for why O(n log n) sorts beat O(n^2) ones and when linear-time sorts apply.
details: Use the VisuAlgo Sorting module and compare merge vs quicksort behavior.
Read the cp-algorithms / GeeksforGeeks sorting notes for the recurrence math.

tasks.title: Master binary search
tasks.summary: Standard search, search on rotated arrays, and binary search on the answer.
tasks.whatYouGain: One template that handles index search and answer search — a disproportionately high-yield skill.

tasks.title: Search & sort problem set
tasks.summary: Five problems spanning plain search, rotated arrays, and search-on-answer.
tasks.whatYouGain: A binary-search reflex that fires whenever the search space is monotonic.

resources.label: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array

stages.title: Trees: Binary Trees & BSTs
stages.summary: Trees combine recursion and structure. You learn traversals (DFS variants and BFS level-order), binary search tree properties, and the recursive patterns that solve most tree problems.
objectives: Traverse trees in preorder, inorder, postorder, and level-order.
Exploit BST ordering for O(log n) search, insert, and validation.
Solve tree problems by defining what each recursive call returns.
mistakes: Confusing BST validation with merely checking parent-child pairs.
Forgetting the null base case in recursive tree functions.
Using global state where a return value would be cleaner.

tasks.title: Binary search trees, visualized
tasks.summary: Watch BST insert, search, delete, and self-balancing (AVL) operations.
tasks.whatYouGain: A solid mental model of ordered trees and why balance keeps operations logarithmic.
details: Use the VisuAlgo BST module; insert values and watch rotations on the AVL variant.
Trace an inorder traversal and confirm it yields sorted output.

tasks.title: Tree traversal & BST problem set
tasks.summary: Five problems across traversal, depth, validation, and ancestors.
tasks.whatYouGain: The 'what does each recursive call return?' habit that makes tree problems tractable.

resources.label: Lowest Common Ancestor of a BST

tasks.title: Build a small BST library
tasks.summary: Implement a BST with insert, search, delete, and inorder traversal from scratch.
tasks.whatYouGain: Proof you understand trees deeply enough to build, not just use, one.
details: Implement the four operations and test against a brute-force sorted list.
Print the tree at each step to verify structure.
checklist: delete handles leaf, one-child, and two-child cases

stages.title: Heaps & Priority Queues
stages.summary: When you repeatedly need the smallest or largest item, a heap gives it in O(log n). Heaps power top-K problems, streaming medians, and Dijkstra's algorithm in the graph stage.
objectives: Use a min-heap and max-heap for top-K and scheduling problems.
Maintain a running statistic (like a median) with two heaps.
Explain heapify and the O(log n) push/pop cost.
mistakes: Sorting the whole array when a heap of size K is enough.
Mixing up min-heap and max-heap (and the negation trick).
Pushing more than needed and losing the O(n log k) advantage.

tasks.title: Binary heap mechanics
tasks.summary: Watch insert, extract-min, and heapify maintain the heap property.
tasks.whatYouGain: A clear picture of the array-backed tree that makes priority queues fast.
details: Use the VisuAlgo Binary Heap module to watch sift-up and sift-down.
Note how a heap is stored compactly in an array.

tasks.title: Heap problem set
tasks.summary: Five problems on top-K, merging, scheduling, and streaming medians.
tasks.whatYouGain: The instinct to reach for a heap whenever 'K largest/smallest' or 'next to process' appears.

resources.label: Kth Largest Element in an Array

stages.title: Graphs
stages.summary: The biggest and most rewarding stage. Graphs model networks, maps, dependencies, and states. You learn representations, BFS/DFS, topological sort, shortest paths, minimum spanning trees, and union-find.
objectives: Represent graphs with adjacency lists and traverse them via BFS and DFS.
Order dependencies with topological sort and detect cycles.
Compute shortest paths (BFS, Dijkstra) and minimum spanning trees (Prim/Kruskal).
Use union-find to track connectivity efficiently.
caseStudies: Course Schedule is dependency resolution as a graph — a topological sort / cycle-detection problem in disguise.
Number of Islands is connected-components counting via flood fill (BFS/DFS).
mistakes: Revisiting nodes because you forgot a visited set.
Using Dijkstra on graphs with negative edges (use Bellman-Ford).
Confusing 'shortest path in unweighted graph' (BFS) with weighted (Dijkstra).

tasks.title: Graph representations & traversal, visualized
tasks.summary: See graph structures and watch BFS and DFS sweep across them.
tasks.whatYouGain: Fluency with adjacency lists and the two traversals that underlie every graph algorithm.
details: Explore the VisuAlgo Graph Structures and Graph Traversal modules.
Read the cp-algorithms BFS and DFS articles for clean reference implementations.

tasks.title: Shortest paths & spanning trees, visualized
tasks.summary: Watch Dijkstra/Bellman-Ford and Prim/Kruskal build paths and trees, then read the theory.
tasks.whatYouGain: A working grasp of the weighted-graph algorithms that show up constantly in interviews and CP.
details: Use the VisuAlgo SSSP and MST modules to watch the algorithms relax edges.
Read the cp-algorithms Dijkstra and Kruskal articles.

tasks.title: Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union)
tasks.summary: Path compression and union by rank make connectivity queries nearly O(1).
tasks.whatYouGain: A go-to structure for connectivity, cycle detection in undirected graphs, and Kruskal's MST.
details: Watch the VisuAlgo Union-Find module, then read the cp-algorithms DSU article.

tasks.title: Graph problem set
tasks.summary: Five problems across flood fill, cloning, topological sort, and shortest path.
tasks.whatYouGain: The ability to model a word problem as a graph and pick the right traversal.

stages.title: Dynamic Programming
stages.summary: The stage everyone fears and everyone needs. DP solves problems by reusing answers to overlapping subproblems. You learn to spot the structure, define a state, write a recurrence, and convert recursion to tabulation.
objectives: Identify overlapping subproblems and optimal substructure.
Define a DP state and recurrence, then memoize it.
Convert top-down memoization to bottom-up tabulation and optimize space.
caseStudies: Coin Change is the canonical unbounded-knapsack-style DP: minimum coins as a recurrence over remaining amount.
Longest Common Subsequence is the template for nearly all two-string DP (edit distance, etc.).
mistakes: Jumping to tabulation before you can state the recurrence.
Wrong or missing base cases in the DP table.
Not recognizing a problem is DP because it looks like greedy.

tasks.title: From recursion to DP
tasks.summary: Revisit the recursion tree to see repeated subproblems, then learn memoization and tabulation.
tasks.whatYouGain: The core insight that DP is just recursion plus a cache — demystifying the whole topic.
details: Reopen the VisuAlgo Recursion module and find the repeated subtrees in Fibonacci.
Read a DP-focused reference (cp-algorithms / GeeksforGeeks) and reproduce one example top-down and bottom-up.

tasks.title: 1-D DP problem set
tasks.summary: Five one-dimensional DP problems to build the state-and-recurrence habit.
tasks.whatYouGain: A repeatable process: define state, write recurrence, set base case, fill the table.

tasks.title: 2-D DP problem set
tasks.summary: Five grid and two-sequence DP problems — the harder, higher-value tier.
tasks.whatYouGain: Comfort with 2-D states, the gateway to most hard DP interview questions.

stages.title: Greedy Algorithms & Intervals
stages.summary: Greedy makes the locally best choice and hopes it's globally optimal — and proving when that's valid is the real skill. Interval scheduling problems are the classic proving ground.
objectives: Recognize when a greedy choice is provably optimal versus when DP is needed.
Sort-then-sweep to solve interval merging and scheduling.
Justify a greedy strategy with an exchange argument.
mistakes: Applying greedy where it gives a wrong answer (should have been DP).
Forgetting to sort before the greedy sweep.
Choosing the wrong sort key (start vs end time).

tasks.title: When greedy works
tasks.summary: Greedy-choice property, exchange arguments, and how interval problems exploit sorting.
tasks.whatYouGain: A way to decide quickly whether to trust a greedy idea or fall back to DP.

tasks.title: Greedy & intervals problem set
tasks.summary: Five problems on jumps, gas, and interval scheduling.
tasks.whatYouGain: The sort-then-sweep template and a feel for which greedy key is correct.

stages.title: Advanced: Tries, Bits & Range Structures
stages.summary: The capstone. You add tries for prefix problems, bit manipulation for compact state, and range-query structures (segment tree, Fenwick tree) plus a string-matching algorithm — the tools that separate strong solvers from average ones.
objectives: Build and use a trie for prefix search and word problems.
Manipulate bits for sets, masks, and constant-space tricks.
Answer range queries with a segment tree or Fenwick tree.
Match patterns in linear time with a string algorithm (KMP / prefix function).
mistakes: Reaching for advanced structures when a hash map suffices.
Sign and overflow bugs in bit manipulation.
Off-by-one errors in segment-tree indexing.

tasks.title: Range-query structures, visualized
tasks.summary: Watch segment trees and Fenwick trees answer and update range queries.
tasks.whatYouGain: Intuition for log-time range sum/min/max with updates — a competitive-programming staple.
details: Use the VisuAlgo Segment Tree and Fenwick Tree modules.
Read the cp-algorithms segment tree and Fenwick articles for implementations.

tasks.title: Bit manipulation & string matching
tasks.summary: Bitmask tricks plus the KMP prefix function for linear-time pattern search.
tasks.whatYouGain: Two specialist tools that crack problems brute force can't touch.
details: Watch the VisuAlgo Bitmask module, then read the cp-algorithms prefix-function (KMP) article.

tasks.title: Advanced problem set
tasks.summary: Five problems across tries, bits, and design.
tasks.whatYouGain: Hands-on reps with the structures that show up in the hardest interview rounds.

resources.label: Design Add and Search Words Data Structure

tasks.title: Capstone: timed mixed mock
tasks.summary: Simulate an interview: solve a random medium and hard under time pressure, then review.
tasks.whatYouGain: Proof the roadmap stuck — you can now identify and apply the right tool cold.
details: Pick one random Medium and one random Hard you haven't seen.
Give yourself 45 minutes total, no hints.
Afterward, write which structure/algorithm each needed and why.
checklist: Stated the approach and complexity before coding
Reached a working solution within the time box
Identified the core pattern for each problem
Wrote down one thing to revisit
